draft.introduction: The Great Hymn to the Aten (c. 1345 BCE) from the tomb of Ay at Amarna is attributed to Pharaoh Akhenaten. It celebrates the sun-disc as the sole creator god and is remarkable for its parallels with Psalm 104.

translation.text: How manifold are your works! They are hidden from the face of man. O sole god, like whom there is no other! You created the earth according to your desire, being alone: all peoples, herds, and flocks. All that is on earth, walking on legs, all that is above, flying with wings.
translation.original_text: wr jr.wt=k, ḥāp(.w) m ḥr. nṬr wā nn kj ḥr-ḫā.t=f. jr.n=k tā m āb=k, tw wā.tj

translator_notes.note: Compare Psalm 104:24: “O Lord, how manifold are your works! In wisdom you have made them all.” Whether the parallel represents direct literary borrowing, a shared cultural milieu, or independent expression of similar theology remains one of the most debated questions in biblical studies.
